description: Create a content slide on nanotechnology evolution with a four-stage timeline/process layout and a right-hand bullet callout sidebar.

header.headline: Nanotechnology is uniquely placed to revolutionize material science and innovation – By 2050, applications could affect every industry and purpose
header.sub: Nanotechnology will evolve today's already commonplace uses to extraordinary future applications

steps.label: Passive nanostructures
steps.sub: This includes nano-materials, -structures or -tubes. Graphene, for example, is a form of carbon, derived from graphite, consisting of a single layer of atoms arranged in a two-dimensional honeycomb lattice nanostructure. Mechanically, it is 100x stronger and 6x lighter than steel, and displays many other unique optical and electronic properties.

steps.label: Active nanostructures
steps.sub: Nanomaterials performing functions and tasks in materials or objects. Nanomedicine, for example, has the potential to cure diseases such as cancer; nanorobots are being developed to navigate in human blood vessels and destroy cancer cells, as artificial immune cells cannot be manipulated by cancer.

steps.label: Nanosystems
steps.sub: Self-assembly of nano-factories that works together with other nanoparticles and machines. In 2018, an MIT engineer created a nanomaterial that can grow, strengthen and repair itself using CO2 from the air. In the future, surface properties could be evolved to become self-healing due to nanotechnology.

steps.label: Molecular nanosystems
steps.sub: Full control of nanosystems able to create structures to complex, atomic specifications including applications for every industry and purpose.

items.text: Projected development of nanotechnologies from past to future, moving from passive nanostructures in the 2000s toward molecular nanosystems by 2050+.
Potential applications expand significantly over time, culminating in broad cross-industry impact.
